svg.text: Byte-level structure with explicit size calculations and dequantization math

g.text: Each box = 1 byte. Calculations show: (weights × bits) / 8 = bytes
High bits: 32 × 1 bit / 8 = 4 bytes (5th bit stored separately)
Weights: 32 × 8 bits / 8 = 32 bytes (1 byte per weight!)
Often used as intermediate for activations
K-Quant Formats (256 weights per block) - Each Has DIFFERENT Formula!
K-Quant Formulas Are ALL DIFFERENT (Critical for Quantization!)
Has: d (FP32 only!). NO sub-scales, NO min!
Simple Asymmetric (Q4_1): min stored as FP16 (can be negative)
weight = q × d + m // m is added, can be negative
weight = q×d×sc - dm×mn // mn is SUBTRACTED (always positive)
Quantization (Encoding) Process is DIFFERENT for Each Format!
To quantize weights, you need format-specific logic:
1. Find block [min, max], compute d, dmin
2. For each sub-block: compute scale, min (6-bit)
3. q = (weight + dmin×mn) / (d×sc) ← ADD min to encode!
2. For each sub-block: compute int8 scale
Q4_K - Primary K-Quant Format (Most Common in GGUF)
Sub-block scales (12 bytes = 8 scales + 8 mins, 6-bit each):
// For weight i in sub-block j (j = i/32):
Note: Q8_K uses FP32 scale (4 bytes), not FP16 (2 bytes)!
This is intentional for higher precision in activation quantization.
Q8_K is typically used to quantize ACTIVATIONS when weights are Q4_K.
This enables Q4_K × Q8_K integer GEMM with VNNI acceleration.
C-Kernel-Engine Quantization Formats Reference | Based on ckernel_quant.h
Each K-quant format uses a DIFFERENT formula for both dequantization and quantization encoding
